summary: Spolsky criticizes the proliferation of popular nonfiction books and blog posts that dress up anecdotes as scientific insights. He uses Michiko Kakutani's review of Malcolm Gladwell's 'Outliers' as a springboard to attack the broader trend of journalists and non-experts writing books that extrapolate sweeping theories from cherry-picked stories. He admits he himself popularized the formula of using amusing anecdotes to prove universal truths, and acknowledges the irony that the same anecdote can be used to prove opposite conclusions. He concludes by calling for more people to write about things they actually know about, rather than packaging entertaining stories as wisdom.
key_points: Popular nonfiction increasingly substitutes entertaining anecdotes for rigorous evidence, with journalists writing authoritatively about subjects they don't deeply understand
The success of writers like Gladwell and Friedman has spawned countless imitators who recycle the same well-known stories to support whatever thesis they're selling
Spolsky self-awarely admits he popularized the same formula of 'tell a funny story, then extrapolate a universal truth' in his own blogging
The same anecdote can be used to prove completely opposite conclusions, which reveals the fundamental weakness of anecdote-driven reasoning
Jeff Atwood's flamewars over NP-completeness illustrate the contrast between vague sociological concepts and rigorous, well-defined fields of knowledge
money_quotes: An unbelievable proliferation of anecdotes disguised as science, self-professed experts writing about things they actually know nothing about, and amusing stories disguised as metaphors for how the world works.
It all becomes insanely popular simply because the stories are fun and interesting and everybody wants to hear a good story.
It's practically designed to get everything wrong, to insure that, no matter how ignorant the reporters are on an issue, they'll find someone who knows even less to write the actual story.
I practically invented the formula of 'tell a funny story and then get all serious and show how this amusing anecdote just goes to show that one thing or the other is a universal truth.'

Six years later, a new king arises who did not know Joel, and he writes up another amusing anecdote, really, it's the same anecdote, and he uses it to prove the exact opposite, and everyone is like, oh yes! how true!
This is not the way to move science forward.
key_insight: Entertaining anecdotes make for popular writing but terrible science, and the same story can be used to prove completely opposite conclusions, which should make us deeply skeptical of anecdote-driven theories.
